pattern: Reduce the 'every subarray of length 3 or more' condition to a purely local rule about every window of exactly 3 consecutive elements, then find the cheapest way to break up long low runs with a small rolling DP.
hints: The condition about every subarray of length 3 or more is equivalent to a much simpler local rule about every window of exactly 3 consecutive elements — work out why a longer subarray is automatically covered once every length-3 window is, then you only ever need to reason about length-3 windows.

Raising one element up to exactly k (never further, since that would only cost more) either costs nothing, if it's already >= k, or costs k minus its value; you are choosing which elements to 'activate' so that no 3 consecutive elements are all inactive.

Scan left to right tracking how many consecutive elements have passed since the last activated one (0, 1, or 2 — a 3rd in a row would break the rule), and at each position take the cheaper of two choices: activate the current element now (pay its cost, reset the streak to 0) or leave it inactive (free, extend the streak by 1, only allowed if that stays under 3).

edge_inputs.description: every element already meets k, so no operations are needed
k = 0 means every element already qualifies
already-qualifying elements are spaced exactly two apart, so no window is ever all-low despite long stretches of zeros
a long uniform run of low values needs several strategically placed boosts, not just one per window
minimum array length with maximum possible k and all-zero values
minimum array length where one element already reaches the maximum k
qualifying elements spaced exactly at the boundary of a length-3 window (gap of two low elements) so no boost is needed
a single well-placed middle boost covers three overlapping windows at once

stress.description: large arrays with roughly half the values forced below k and half fully random, to exercise long low runs and scattered qualifying elements
